description: This is an optical micrograph showing the microstructure of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after homogenization heat treatment at 1100 °C for 4 h, with a scale bar of 40 μm. It presents a dual-phase structure: the continuous light-colored matrix is BCC ferrite, and the dispersed gray irregular precipitates (including granular, elongated, and irregular shaped particles) are Fe₃AlCₓ (K-phase) carbides uniformly distributed in the ferrite matrix. This figure provides the baseline initial microstructure of the as-prepared alloy before high-temperature oxidation tests, confirming the expected dual-phase constituent is obtained after heat treatment, and serves as a reference for subsequent microstructure comparison after oxidation at 600-900 °C in dry air.

This figure presents the isothermal oxidation thermogravimetric curves of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy tested in dry air at four temperatures: 600℃, 700℃, 800℃, and 900℃. The x-axis represents oxidation time in the range of 0 to 30 h, and the y-axis denotes weight gain per unit area with a range from 0 to 2 mg/cm². The curve at 700℃ shows rapid initial weight gain, reaching a plateau of ~1.5 mg/cm² after ~15 h. The 600℃ curve keeps a nearly linear rising trend, reaching ~1.2 mg/cm² at 25 h without plateauing. Curves at 800℃ and 900°C show much lower final weight gain, plateauing at ~0.65 mg/cm² and ~0.35 mg/cm² respectively after 10-20 h of oxidation. The results indicate that oxidation resistance of the alloy increases with temperature from 700℃ to 900℃ due to the formation of protective Al-rich oxide scales, while 600°C oxidation suffers from accelerated kinetics related to internal oxidation.

This is an optical metallographic cross-sectional image of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after 24 h isothermal oxidation in dry air at 600 °C, with a scale bar of 50 μm. The dark region at the top corresponds to the space outside the sample surface, the discontinuous dark raised structures attached to the sample surface are oxidation nodules, and the bright region at the bottom is the alloy matrix with dispersed second-phase Fe₃AlCₓ (K-phase) precipitates. The morphology shows that oxidation nodules have irregular interfaces with the alloy matrix. This figure confirms the formation of surface oxidation nodules under 600 °C oxidation, supporting the core conclusion that internal oxidation occurs beneath these nodules, leading to a dramatic oxidation rate increase after the initial parabolic oxidation stage at 600 °C.

This is a cross-sectional scanning electron micrograph of the oxide scale formed on Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after 96 h isothermal oxidation at 600 °C in dry air, with a scale bar of 10 μm. Three distinct regions are observed: the top outermost porous Fe-rich oxide layer with abundant pore defects, a middle relatively dense mixed Fe-Al oxide layer, and the bottom ferrite matrix dispersed with Fe₃AlCₓ precipitates. Obvious internal oxidation features are identified at the interface between the oxide scale and the alloy matrix, confirming that internal oxidation occurs under 600 °C oxidation condition for this alloy. This morphology evidence supports the conclusion that oxidation at 600 °C cannot form a continuous protective Al₂O₃ scale, leading to accelerated oxidation after the initial parabolic oxidation stage, which is different from the oxidation behavior at 700-900 °C where no internal oxidation is detected.

This figure presents the EPMA line scan elemental concentration profile across the oxide scale and alloy matrix of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after 96 h isothermal oxidation at 600 °C in dry air. The x-axis denotes distance from the sample surface in μm, while the y-axis refers to alloy element content in wt%. Two element curves are included: Fe content reaches 91.8 wt% in the bulk alloy (distance > 60 μm, defined as Alloy region), and fluctuates in the oxide region (0 to ~60 μm) with a peak value of 67.2 wt% and valley values of 43 wt% and 53.9 wt%; Al content stays at 7.5 wt% in the bulk alloy, and exhibits a peak of 14.5 wt% at 30 to 55 μm in the oxide layer, corresponding to Al-rich internal oxidation products. A dashed line marks the oxide/alloy interface. This profile verifies the three-layered oxide structure formed at 600 °C, supporting the conclusion that internal oxidation occurs for the studied alloy under this oxidation condition.

This is an optical metallographic cross-sectional micrograph of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after 24 h isothermal oxidation in dry air at 700 °C, with a scale bar of 50 μm. The dark upper region is the mounting resin, the intermediate light grey porous layer is the formed oxide scale with visible surface nodules, and the lower white region is the alloy matrix containing dispersed Fe₃AlCₓ (K-phase) precipitates. No internal oxidation products or decarburization features are observed in the alloy matrix beneath the oxide scale, consistent with reported characterization results. The oxide scale consists of an outer porous Fe-rich oxide layer and an inner Al-rich oxide layer, as confirmed by supporting EPMA data. This figure supports the conclusion that oxidation at 700 °C enables sufficient Al diffusion to form a continuous inner oxide layer that suppresses internal oxidation, which differs from the oxidation behavior at 600 °C where internal oxidation occurs.

This is an optical metallographic micrograph of the cross-section of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after 24 h isothermal oxidation in dry air at 900 °C, with a scale bar of 200 μm. The dark region on the top and left is the external environment/embedding material, while the light region is the etched alloy matrix with visible fine microstructural features. No oxide nodules or internal oxidation defects are observed at the oxidized surface, which is distinct from morphologies obtained at lower temperatures: internal oxidation occurs at 600 °C, abundant nodules form at 700 °C, and few small nodules exist at 800 °C. This observation confirms that sufficient Al atom mobility at 900 °C enables the formation of a continuous protective Al₂O₃ or spinel oxide scale, suppressing internal oxidation and iron-rich nodule growth, supporting the conclusion that the oxidation resistance of the alloy increases as oxidation temperature rises from 600 °C to 900 °C.
